The enlargement may be slightly less than, or asymmetric to, what true central (FSH-driven) maturation would produce, but it is enough to resemble central precocity — which is exactly why the LH level, not testicular size alone, is needed to localise the lesion.
keyPoints: Suppressed gonadotropins but enlarged testes (> 4 mL) — a mimic of central puberty.
Massive intratesticular testosterone paracrine-drives tubule expansion without FSH.
Testicular size alone can mislead; the LH level is the localiser.

items.title: LH-secreting pituitary adenoma: the central overdrive (GDPP)
items.content: A functional LH-secreting gonadotropinoma is a monoclonal neoplastic proliferation of pituitary gonadotroph cells that autonomously secrete intact, bioactive LH (often with variable FSH or isolated alpha-subunits). The tumour operates outside normal negative feedback, so its high continuous LH chronically stimulates the Leydig cells to make testosterone. The biochemical signature is high testosterone with inappropriately elevated or unsuppressed LH — the tumour ignores the high testosterone that would normally shut LH off. Clinically there is bilateral, symmetric testicular enlargement (true central maturation, helped by co-secreted FSH/intact axis drive), and there may be mass-effect symptoms from a macroadenoma (bitemporal hemianopsia, headaches). These tumours are exceedingly rare in prepubertal children but are the vital conceptual contrast to testotoxicosis. Treatment is transsphenoidal surgical resection, sometimes with somatostatin analogs.
keyPoints: Neoplastic gonadotrophs autonomously secrete intact bioactive LH (± FSH/alpha-subunit).
Biochemistry: high testosterone with inappropriately elevated/unsuppressed LH.
Bilateral symmetric testes + possible mass effect; treat by transsphenoidal resection ± somatostatin analogs.

items.title: How the LH level differentiates central from peripheral virilization (the boolean operator)
items.content: In a virilizing boy with high testosterone, the LH acts as a boolean operator that localises the lesion. A suppressed LH (typically < 0.3 IU/L) points strictly to the periphery — gonadotropin-independent causes such as testotoxicosis, congenital adrenal hyperplasia, or a Leydig cell tumour — because autonomous peripheral androgen has shut the axis down. An elevated or inappropriately unsuppressed LH points squarely to the central axis — an LH-secreting adenoma, a hypothalamic hamartoma, or idiopathic central activation — because the drive is coming from above. So the diagnostic algorithm in the virilized boy begins with: is the LH suppressed or elevated?
keyPoints: High testosterone + suppressed LH (< 0.3) → peripheral (testotoxicosis, CAH, Leydig tumour).
High testosterone + elevated/unsuppressed LH → central (adenoma, hamartoma, idiopathic).
Start the work-up of a virilized boy by asking whether LH is suppressed or elevated.

items.title: Testotoxicosis vs LH-secreting adenoma: comparative summary
items.content: Node of failure: testotoxicosis is peripheral (Leydig-cell LHCGR receptor); the adenoma is central (pituitary gonadotrophs). Category: GIPP (gonadotropin-independent) vs GDPP (gonadotropin-dependent). Testosterone: markedly elevated in both. LH: profoundly suppressed (< 0.3 IU/L) in testotoxicosis versus elevated or inappropriately normal with the adenoma. FSH: suppressed in testotoxicosis versus variable (often elevated/normal) with the adenoma. Testicular size: enlarged but possibly slightly asymmetric/smaller than expected for adult Tanner V in testotoxicosis, versus symmetrically enlarged with true central maturation in the adenoma. Pathogenesis: genetic gain-of-function LHCGR mutation versus a neoplastic pituitary tumour. Primary treatment: androgen-receptor blocker (bicalutamide) plus aromatase inhibitor (anastrozole) versus transsphenoidal resection (± somatostatin analogs).
keyPoints: Peripheral receptor (GIPP) vs central gonadotroph tumour (GDPP).
Suppressed LH/FSH vs elevated/inappropriate LH (variable FSH).
AR blocker + aromatase inhibitor vs transsphenoidal surgery.

items.question: Why is the LH level the single most useful test to localize the cause in a virilized boy with high testosterone?
items.answer: Because LH directly reports whether the androgen drive is central or peripheral. If the source of testosterone is autonomous and peripheral — an activated Leydig-cell receptor (testotoxicosis), adrenal androgen excess (CAH), or a Leydig cell tumour — the high testosterone feeds back and suppresses pituitary LH to very low, flat levels, so a suppressed LH localises the problem below the pituitary. If instead the drive is central — an LH-secreting adenoma, a hypothalamic hamartoma, or idiopathic central activation — LH is itself the driver and is elevated or inappropriately unsuppressed despite the high testosterone. Because the two categories produce opposite LH results from an identical clinical picture, LH acts as a clean binary switch that immediately routes the work-up toward the periphery or the centre.
keyPoints: Suppressed LH = peripheral autonomous androgen (testotoxicosis, CAH, Leydig tumour).
Elevated/unsuppressed LH = central drive (adenoma, hamartoma, idiopathic).
Identical phenotype, opposite LH — a clean binary localiser.

items.question: How do the gonadal findings and treatments differ between testotoxicosis and an LH-secreting adenoma?
items.answer: Gonadal findings: testotoxicosis enlarges the testes through massive intratesticular testosterone acting on the tubules despite suppressed FSH, so they grow but may be slightly asymmetric or smaller than expected for an adult Tanner V; an LH-secreting adenoma drives true central, symmetric, bilateral testicular maturation (helped by co-secreted FSH and the intact axis), and may add mass-effect signs such as headaches or bitemporal hemianopsia. Treatment follows the node of failure: testotoxicosis is gonadotropin-independent, so GnRH analogs do not work and therapy targets the periphery — an androgen-receptor blocker such as bicalutamide combined with an aromatase inhibitor such as anastrozole to block androgen action and limit estrogen-driven bone maturation; the adenoma is a central tumour treated by transsphenoidal surgical resection, sometimes with somatostatin analogs. The LH result that distinguishes them also dictates the entire therapeutic strategy.
keyPoints: Testotoxicosis: testes enlarge (paracrine testosterone), may be asymmetric; adenoma: symmetric central maturation + possible mass effect.
Testotoxicosis (GIPP): GnRH analogs fail → AR blocker (bicalutamide) + aromatase inhibitor (anastrozole).
Adenoma (GDPP, tumoral): transsphenoidal resection ± somatostatin analogs.

items.stem: What molecular lesion causes familial testotoxicosis (familial male-limited precocious puberty)?
options: An activating (gain-of-function) LHCGR mutation locking the LH receptor 'on'
items.explanation: Testotoxicosis is caused by an activating LHCGR mutation: the LH receptor is constitutively active, so Leydig cells make testosterone autonomously without LH — a peripheral, gonadotropin-independent cause.

items.stem: What is the biochemical signature of familial testotoxicosis?
options: High testosterone with elevated LH and FSH
High testosterone with profoundly suppressed, flat LH and FSH
items.explanation: Autonomous Leydig-cell testosterone production feeds back to suppress the axis, giving markedly elevated testosterone with profoundly suppressed (flat) LH and FSH — the hallmark of gonadotropin-independent precocity.

items.stem: Why can the testes enlarge in testotoxicosis despite suppressed gonadotropins?
options: Massive intratesticular (paracrine) testosterone drives some seminiferous-tubule expansion even without FSH
items.explanation: The constitutively active LH receptor generates enormous local testosterone, which paracrine-drives tubule expansion without FSH — producing testicular enlargement that can mimic central puberty.

items.stem: What is the biochemical signature of an LH-secreting pituitary adenoma causing precocity?
options: High testosterone with inappropriately elevated or unsuppressed LH
Normal testosterone with high FSH only
items.explanation: The tumour secretes LH autonomously, ignoring negative feedback, so testosterone is high yet LH is elevated or inappropriately unsuppressed — a central, gonadotropin-dependent picture.

items.stem: In a virilized boy with markedly high testosterone, which single result best localizes the cause?
options: The LH level — suppressed points peripheral, elevated/unsuppressed points central
items.explanation: LH is the boolean differentiator: high testosterone with suppressed LH indicates a peripheral source (testotoxicosis, CAH, Leydig tumour), while high testosterone with elevated/unsuppressed LH indicates central drive (adenoma, hamartoma, idiopathic).

items.stem: What is the primary pharmacological treatment for familial testotoxicosis?
options: An androgen-receptor blocker (e.g. bicalutamide) plus an aromatase inhibitor (e.g. anastrozole)
items.explanation: Because testotoxicosis is gonadotropin-independent, GnRH analogs are ineffective. Treatment targets the periphery: an androgen-receptor blocker to oppose androgen action plus an aromatase inhibitor to limit estrogen-driven epiphyseal fusion.

items.stem: What is the primary treatment for an LH-secreting pituitary adenoma?
options: Transsphenoidal surgical resection (sometimes with somatostatin analogs)
items.explanation: An LH-secreting gonadotropinoma is a pituitary tumour, so the primary treatment is transsphenoidal surgical resection, with somatostatin analogs as an adjunct in some cases.

items.stem: A virilized boy has high testosterone with a suppressed LH (< 0.3 IU/L). Which differential fits?
options: Testotoxicosis, congenital adrenal hyperplasia, or a Leydig cell tumour
items.explanation: A suppressed LH with high testosterone indicates a peripheral, gonadotropin-independent source — testotoxicosis, CAH, or a Leydig cell tumour. Central causes (adenoma, hamartoma, idiopathic) would show an elevated/unsuppressed LH.

items.stem: Why are GnRH analogs ineffective in familial testotoxicosis?
options: The dose required is too high
The disorder is gonadotropin-independent — the LH receptor is autonomously active, so suppressing already-low LH changes nothing
They are only effective in boys over 12
items.explanation: GnRH analogs work by suppressing gonadotropin secretion, but in testotoxicosis LH is already suppressed and the Leydig-cell receptor is driven autonomously, so there is nothing for a GnRH analog to act on.

items.statement: Familial testotoxicosis is caused by an activating (gain-of-function) mutation in the LH receptor (LHCGR).
items.explanation: True. The constitutively active LH receptor drives autonomous Leydig-cell testosterone production independent of LH.

items.statement: In familial testotoxicosis, LH and FSH are elevated.
items.explanation: False. Autonomous peripheral testosterone suppresses the axis, so LH and FSH are profoundly suppressed (flat).

items.statement: Testes can enlarge in testotoxicosis despite suppressed gonadotropins because of high intratesticular (paracrine) testosterone.
items.explanation: True. The massive local testosterone drives some tubular expansion even without FSH, which can mimic central puberty.

items.statement: An LH-secreting pituitary adenoma shows high testosterone with inappropriately elevated or unsuppressed LH.
items.explanation: True. The tumour secretes LH autonomously and ignores the negative feedback that high testosterone would normally exert.

items.statement: In a virilized boy with high testosterone, a suppressed LH points to a peripheral (gonadotropin-independent) cause.
items.explanation: True. Suppressed LH localises the androgen source below the pituitary — testotoxicosis, CAH, or a Leydig cell tumour.

items.statement: GnRH analogs are the first-line treatment for familial testotoxicosis.
items.explanation: False. Testotoxicosis is gonadotropin-independent, so GnRH analogs do not work; treatment uses an androgen-receptor blocker plus an aromatase inhibitor.

items.assertion: GnRH analogs are ineffective for familial testotoxicosis.
items.reason: Testotoxicosis is gonadotropin-independent: an autonomously active LH receptor drives the Leydig cells regardless of the already-suppressed LH.
items.explanation: Both statements are true and the reason explains the assertion. Since GnRH analogs act by lowering gonadotropins, and in testotoxicosis LH is already suppressed while the receptor is autonomously active, there is no gonadotropin pathway for the analog to block — so it cannot work.

items.title: Why testotoxicosis breaks the standard precocious-puberty algorithm
items.content: The standard work-up of precocious puberty is binary: pubertal LH/FSH with a brisk GnRH response means central (gonadotropin-dependent) precocious puberty; suppressed gonadotropins with no GnRH response means peripheral (gonadotropin-independent). Testotoxicosis violently breaks this. It is gonadotropin-INDEPENDENT (so LH and FSH are suppressed), yet the testes are BILATERALLY ENLARGED — a finding everyone associates with central puberty — and it causes true, progressive virilization indistinguishable from central precocity. The result is a paradoxical triad seen in no other peripheral cause: suppressed gonadotropins + symmetric testicular enlargement + progressive puberty. Recognising that this paradox exists is the first step to not being trapped by it.
keyPoints: Standard algorithm: pubertal LH = central; suppressed LH = peripheral.
Testotoxicosis is peripheral (suppressed LH) BUT has bilaterally enlarged testes + true virilization.
Paradoxical triad: suppressed gonadotropins + symmetric testicular enlargement + progressive puberty.

items.title: How testotoxicosis differs from other peripheral precocious puberty
items.content: Among gonadotropin-independent causes, testicular findings and a few targeted tests separate them. Testotoxicosis: autonomous testicular testosterone, BILATERALLY and symmetrically ENLARGED testes, normal 17-OHP and hCG, spermatogenesis often present. CAH: adrenal androgens (ACTH-driven), testes remain PREPUBERTAL/small, with ELEVATED 17-OHP and androstenedione (and salt-wasting/hyperpigmentation clues). hCG-secreting tumour: testes bilaterally enlarged but with an ELEVATED hCG and a tumour source (hepatomegaly/mediastinal mass). Leydig cell tumour: a UNILATERAL palpable testicular mass (asymmetric). Exogenous androgens: small soft testes with a history of exposure (creams, gels, supplements). The differentiating triad for testotoxicosis is therefore symmetric bilateral testicular enlargement + suppressed gonadotropins + no exogenous hCG or androgen source.
keyPoints: Testotoxicosis: symmetric bilateral enlargement, normal 17-OHP and hCG.
CAH: small testes + high 17-OHP; Leydig tumour: unilateral mass; hCG tumour: high hCG; exogenous: small testes + exposure history.
Triad for testotoxicosis: symmetric bilateral enlargement + suppressed gonadotropins + no exogenous hCG/androgen.

items.title: How testotoxicosis mimics central precocious puberty — and the GnRH-agonist trap
items.content: This is the crucial clinical trap. Testotoxicosis and central precocious puberty (CPP) both show progressive penile growth, pubarche, bilateral testicular enlargement, accelerated growth, and advanced bone age — a near-identical surface. They separate biochemically: in testotoxicosis basal LH is suppressed (< 0.1 IU/L), the GnRH stimulation test is flat (no LH response), FSH is suppressed, the brain MRI is normal, onset is often very young (2-4 years), and there is a strong autosomal-dominant family history (father often affected). In CPP the basal LH is pubertal (≥ 0.3, often higher) with a robust LH-predominant GnRH response, FSH is pubertal, and MRI may reveal a CNS lesion. The pitfall: assuming 'bilateral enlargement = CPP' and prescribing a GnRH agonist, which fails completely because the disease is downstream of the pituitary — a documented, repeated error.
keyPoints: Surface is near-identical: penile growth, pubarche, bilateral testes, advanced bone age.
Testotoxicosis: basal LH < 0.1, FLAT GnRH response, normal MRI, very young onset, AD family history.
Trap: 'bilateral enlargement = CPP' → a GnRH agonist that fails completely.

items.title: The molecular lesion: an activating LHCGR mutation
items.content: Testotoxicosis is caused by a heterozygous, gain-of-function (activating) missense mutation in LHCGR (the LH/choriogonadotropin receptor gene) on chromosome 2p21. More than 30 different activating mutations have been described, most clustering in exon 11, which encodes the transmembrane domain of this G-protein-coupled receptor. Inheritance is autosomal dominant with male-limited expression — heterozygous females are asymptomatic carriers, because LH-receptor activation in the ovary alone (without FSH co-activation) produces no phenotype. So a single activating allele, inherited from an affected father (or new), is sufficient to switch the receptor on in males.
keyPoints: Heterozygous activating (gain-of-function) LHCGR missense mutation, chromosome 2p21.
>30 mutations, mostly in exon 11 (transmembrane domain).
Autosomal dominant, male-limited expression (females are asymptomatic carriers).

items.title: How a constitutively active LH receptor drives testosterone
items.content: Normally the LH receptor, a Gs-coupled GPCR on Leydig cells, is active only when LH binds: ligand binding activates Gs-alpha, which stimulates adenylyl cyclase to raise cAMP, activating protein kinase A (PKA); PKA phosphorylates StAR (steroidogenic acute regulatory protein) and steroidogenic enzymes, transporting cholesterol into mitochondria for testosterone synthesis. In prepubertal boys LH is very low, so testosterone is minimal. The activating mutation puts the receptor into an active conformation WITHOUT LH bound, so cAMP and the entire steroidogenic cascade run continuously regardless of (suppressed) extracellular LH — and the Leydig cells pour out testosterone autonomously. This is mechanistically analogous to activating TSH-receptor mutations in toxic thyroid adenomas, which produce thyroid hormone without TSH.
keyPoints: Normal: LH → Gs-alpha → cAMP → PKA → StAR → cholesterol transport → testosterone.
Mutation: receptor active without ligand → constitutive cAMP and steroidogenesis despite suppressed LH.
Analogous to activating TSH-receptor mutations in toxic thyroid adenomas.

items.title: Why the testes enlarge: Leydig hyperplasia plus testosterone-driven spermatogenesis
items.content: Two mechanisms enlarge the testes and explain the central mimicry. First, Leydig-cell hyperplasia/hypertrophy: constitutive LH-receptor activation makes the Leydig cells proliferate and enlarge, increasing testicular volume — not seen in CAH, where testes stay small. Second, intratesticular testosterone-driven spermatogenesis: local testosterone within the seminiferous tubules reaches concentrations 100-1000 times serum levels, and this can partially bypass the usual FSH requirement to initiate spermatogenesis. The consequences are that the testes are bilaterally enlarged (not just a Leydig nodule), spermatogenesis may occur, and fertility potential can be preserved — unlike CAH, where intratesticular testosterone is not elevated and the testes remain small.
keyPoints: Leydig-cell hyperplasia/hypertrophy from constitutive activation enlarges the testes.
Intratesticular testosterone (100-1000x serum) can drive spermatogenesis despite low FSH.
Result: bilateral enlargement and possible preserved fertility (contrast CAH's small testes).

items.title: Why female carriers are asymptomatic: the two-cell, two-gonadotropin paradox
items.content: Heterozygous mothers carrying the same activating LHCGR mutation are completely asymptomatic — no precocious puberty, normal fertility — which is why the condition is male-limited. The reason is the ovary's two-cell, two-gonadotropin system: theca cells use LH to make androgens, but granulosa cells require FSH to drive aromatase and convert those androgens to estradiol. An activated LH receptor alone, without concomitant FSH-receptor drive, produces only small amounts of androgen that are efficiently aromatized, generating no estrogen surge, no follicular maturation, and no clinical phenotype. The Leydig cell, by contrast, produces the final active hormone (testosterone) directly from LH-receptor signalling, so a single activated receptor is sufficient in the male. Understanding this perfectly explains the male-limited inheritance.
keyPoints: Ovary needs two cells/two gonadotropins: theca (LH→androgen) + granulosa (FSH→aromatase→estradiol).
LH-receptor activation alone (no FSH) yields minimal, aromatized androgen → no phenotype.
Leydig cells make the active hormone directly → one activated receptor suffices in males.

items.title: How to treat testotoxicosis: the multi-drug, mechanism-based approach
items.content: Because the disease is entirely downstream of the pituitary, GnRH agonists are ineffective, and treatment targets androgen production and action directly. The standard combination blocks the pathway at several points: an aromatase inhibitor (anastrozole or letrozole) to block conversion of testosterone to estradiol, slowing bone-age advancement and preserving adult height; an anti-androgen (bicalutamide, spironolactone, or flutamide) to block the androgen receptor and limit virilization; and, as a second-line steroidogenesis inhibitor, ketoconazole (CYP17A1 inhibition, used with liver monitoring), with abiraterone an emerging more potent option. A common regimen is ketoconazole plus bicalutamide plus an aromatase inhibitor. The goals are to block testosterone action, slow virilization, preserve height potential, protect fertility (spermatogenesis continues on intratesticular testosterone), and provide psychological/family support.
keyPoints: GnRH agonists do NOT work; treat the periphery directly.
Aromatase inhibitor (anastrozole/letrozole) preserves height; anti-androgen (bicalutamide/spironolactone) blocks virilization.
Ketoconazole (CYP17A1, 2nd line, monitor liver); abiraterone emerging. Goals: block T, slow virilization, preserve height/fertility, support.

items.title: Clinical recognition pearls for testotoxicosis
items.content: Key teaching points for the bedside. A boy with precocious puberty, suppressed LH, and bilaterally enlarged testes has testotoxicosis until proven otherwise — the combination is nearly pathognomonic but is missed if you stop at 'suppressed LH = peripheral'. GnRH agonists will not work, so recognising the diagnosis avoids futile, expensive therapy and directs to the right drugs. Always ask about the father's pubertal timing — the autosomal-dominant inheritance means he was often early himself. The testes are smooth, firm, and symmetrically enlarged, which distinguishes them from the asymmetric, nodular mass of a Leydig cell tumour. Bone age is markedly advanced at presentation, and without treatment adult height is severely compromised. Counsel families: 50% of sons are affected, daughters are asymptomatic carriers.
keyPoints: Suppressed LH + bilaterally enlarged testes = testotoxicosis until proven otherwise.
GnRH agonists fail; ask the father's pubertal history; testes are smooth/symmetric (not a nodular mass).
Advanced bone age threatens height; counsel — 50% of sons affected, daughters asymptomatic carriers.

items.question: Why does testotoxicosis produce the paradoxical combination of suppressed gonadotropins with bilaterally enlarged testes?
items.answer: Because the two findings come from different levels of the axis acting at once. The autonomous testosterone made by the constitutively active Leydig-cell LH receptor feeds back on the hypothalamus and pituitary and suppresses LH and FSH to prepubertal levels — hence suppressed gonadotropins, marking it as gonadotropin-independent. At the same time, that same constitutive receptor activation enlarges the testes by two local mechanisms: Leydig-cell hyperplasia and hypertrophy, and intratesticular testosterone concentrations (100-1000 times serum) high enough to drive seminiferous-tubule expansion and spermatogenesis even without FSH. So the periphery is simultaneously shutting the axis down (suppressed LH/FSH) and growing the gonad (enlarged testes) — a combination no other peripheral cause reproduces, which is exactly why it mimics central puberty and traps the unwary.
keyPoints: Autonomous testosterone suppresses LH/FSH (gonadotropin-independent).
The same receptor activation causes Leydig hyperplasia and very high intratesticular testosterone.
Suppressed gonadotropins + enlarged testes coexist — the central-mimic trap.

items.question: How does the treatment of testotoxicosis differ mechanistically from CPP, and what is the standard regimen?
items.answer: In central precocious puberty the drive comes from the pituitary, so a GnRH agonist suppresses gonadotropins and turns the whole axis off — first-line and highly effective. In testotoxicosis the pathology is downstream of the pituitary (an autonomously active Leydig-cell receptor with already-suppressed LH), so a GnRH agonist has nothing to act on and fails. Treatment must therefore attack androgen production and action directly: an aromatase inhibitor (anastrozole or letrozole) to block testosterone-to-estradiol conversion and slow epiphyseal fusion (preserving adult height); an anti-androgen (bicalutamide, spironolactone, or flutamide) to block the androgen receptor and halt virilization; and a steroidogenesis inhibitor such as ketoconazole (CYP17A1, second-line, with liver monitoring), with abiraterone emerging. A standard combination is ketoconazole plus bicalutamide plus an aromatase inhibitor, with the goals of blocking testosterone action, slowing virilization, preserving height and fertility, and supporting the child and family.
keyPoints: CPP: GnRH agonist works (central drive). Testotoxicosis: GnRH agonist fails (peripheral, suppressed LH).
Treat directly: aromatase inhibitor (height) + anti-androgen (virilization) ± ketoconazole (steroidogenesis).
Standard combo: ketoconazole + bicalutamide + an aromatase inhibitor.

items.stem: Which combination is the paradoxical, near-pathognomonic picture of testotoxicosis?
options: Suppressed gonadotropins with bilaterally and symmetrically enlarged testes and progressive virilization
Suppressed gonadotropins with a unilateral testicular mass
Pubertal LH with a brisk GnRH response
items.explanation: Testotoxicosis uniquely combines suppressed gonadotropins (gonadotropin-independent) with symmetric bilateral testicular enlargement and true progressive virilization — a paradox no other peripheral cause reproduces.

items.stem: Which feature best distinguishes testotoxicosis from congenital adrenal hyperplasia in a virilized boy?
options: Testotoxicosis has bilaterally enlarged testes with normal 17-OHP, whereas CAH has prepubertal/small testes with elevated 17-OHP
CAH has a unilateral testicular mass
items.explanation: In testotoxicosis the testes are enlarged and 17-OHP is normal; in CAH the androgens are adrenal, the testes stay small/prepubertal, and 17-OHP (and androstenedione) are elevated.

items.stem: A virilized boy with suppressed LH has a single firm, nodular, asymmetric testicular mass. What is most likely?
items.explanation: A unilateral, nodular, asymmetric testicular mass suggests a Leydig cell tumour. Testotoxicosis produces smooth, firm, symmetric bilateral enlargement.

items.stem: Why is prescribing a GnRH agonist for a boy with bilateral testicular enlargement a classic pitfall?
options: GnRH agonists worsen all precocious puberty
If the cause is testotoxicosis (gonadotropin-independent), the GnRH agonist fails completely because the disease is downstream of the pituitary
GnRH agonists are too expensive to ever use
Bilateral enlargement always means a tumour
items.explanation: Bilateral enlargement is assumed to indicate central precocious puberty, but in testotoxicosis the LH receptor is autonomously active with suppressed LH, so a GnRH agonist has nothing to suppress and is ineffective — a repeatedly documented error.

items.stem: What is the inheritance pattern and expression of the LHCGR mutation in testotoxicosis?
options: Autosomal dominant with male-limited expression (females are asymptomatic carriers)
items.explanation: The activating LHCGR mutation is autosomal dominant with male-limited expression; heterozygous females are asymptomatic carriers because ovarian LH-receptor activation alone (without FSH) produces no phenotype.

items.stem: How does the activating LHCGR mutation produce testosterone without LH?
options: The receptor adopts an active conformation without ligand, driving constitutive cAMP/PKA/StAR signalling and steroidogenesis
items.explanation: The mutation locks the receptor 'on' without LH, so Gs-alpha → adenylyl cyclase → cAMP → PKA → StAR signalling runs constitutively, producing testosterone independent of (suppressed) LH.

items.stem: Why are female heterozygous carriers of the activating LHCGR mutation asymptomatic?
options: The mutation is silenced in females
Ovarian estradiol production requires FSH-driven granulosa-cell aromatization, so LH-receptor activation alone produces no phenotype (the two-cell, two-gonadotropin system)
Females do not express the LH receptor
items.explanation: In the ovary, theca cells use LH to make androgens but granulosa cells need FSH to aromatize them to estradiol; an activated LH receptor alone yields minimal, efficiently-aromatized androgen and no clinical effect — explaining the male-limited expression.

items.stem: Which regimen reflects the standard mechanism-based treatment of testotoxicosis?
options: An aromatase inhibitor plus an anti-androgen (± a steroidogenesis inhibitor such as ketoconazole)
items.explanation: Because GnRH agonists fail, treatment combines an aromatase inhibitor (to preserve height by slowing bone maturation), an anti-androgen (to block virilization), and sometimes a steroidogenesis inhibitor (ketoconazole), e.g. ketoconazole + bicalutamide + anastrozole.

items.stem: What is the specific purpose of the aromatase inhibitor in treating testotoxicosis?
options: To block conversion of testosterone to estradiol, slowing bone-age advancement and preserving adult height
items.explanation: Estradiol (from aromatization of the high testosterone) drives epiphyseal maturation; an aromatase inhibitor blocks this conversion, slowing bone-age advancement and protecting adult height.

items.statement: In congenital adrenal hyperplasia, the testes are typically enlarged just as in testotoxicosis.
items.explanation: False. In CAH the androgens are adrenal and the testes remain prepubertal/small (with elevated 17-OHP), whereas testotoxicosis bilaterally enlarges the testes.

items.statement: A unilateral, nodular testicular mass favours a Leydig cell tumour over testotoxicosis.
items.explanation: True. Testotoxicosis gives smooth, symmetric, bilateral enlargement; a unilateral nodular mass suggests a Leydig cell tumour.

items.statement: Aromatase inhibitors help preserve adult height in testotoxicosis by delaying epiphyseal fusion.
items.explanation: True. By blocking testosterone-to-estradiol conversion they slow estrogen-driven bone maturation and epiphyseal fusion, preserving height potential.

items.statement: Female heterozygous carriers of the activating LHCGR mutation are typically asymptomatic.
items.explanation: True. Ovarian estrogen output requires FSH-driven aromatization, so LH-receptor activation alone causes no phenotype — hence male-limited expression.

items.statement: Spermatogenesis can occur in testotoxicosis because very high intratesticular testosterone can partly bypass the FSH requirement.
items.explanation: True. Intratesticular testosterone reaches 100-1000x serum levels, which can drive spermatogenesis despite low FSH, so fertility potential may be preserved.

items.statement: GnRH agonists effectively suppress testosterone in testotoxicosis.
items.explanation: False. Testotoxicosis is gonadotropin-independent with already-suppressed LH and an autonomously active receptor, so GnRH agonists have nothing to act on and are ineffective.

items.assertion: Female heterozygous carriers of an activating LHCGR mutation are asymptomatic.
items.reason: Ovarian estradiol production requires FSH-driven granulosa-cell aromatization, so isolated LH-receptor activation produces no estrogenic phenotype (the two-cell, two-gonadotropin system).
items.explanation: Both statements are true and the reason explains the assertion. Because the ovary needs both LH (theca androgens) and FSH (granulosa aromatization) to make estradiol, an activated LH receptor alone is insufficient to cause puberty — which is exactly why female carriers are asymptomatic and the disorder is male-limited.

items.title: Why McCune-Albright causes gonadotropin-independent precocious puberty
items.content: MAS is caused by a somatic activating mutation in GNAS (chromosome 20q13.3), most often R201H or R201C (~85-90%), which abolishes the intrinsic GTPase 'off switch' of Gs-alpha. Gs-alpha is therefore locked active, driving constitutive adenylyl cyclase and cAMP without any ligand or receptor — the signal originates inside the cell, downstream of all receptors. In ovarian granulosa cells this constitutive cAMP drives aromatase and estradiol production independently of FSH, so the ovary makes estrogen autonomously and the high estradiol suppresses LH and FSH to prepubertal levels. The hallmark is a precocious pubertal phenotype with suppressed gonadotropins (peripheral / GIPP).
keyPoints: Somatic activating GNAS mutation (R201H/C) abolishes Gs-alpha GTPase → constitutive cAMP.
Ligand- and receptor-independent: signal originates inside the cell.
Granulosa cells make estradiol autonomously (FSH-independent) → suppressed LH/FSH.

items.title: Why MAS is mosaic, not inherited
items.content: The GNAS mutation is post-zygotic and somatic, so only the cells descended from the originally mutated clone are affected — MAS is a mosaic disease. This explains the patchy, asymmetric involvement (unilateral 'coast of Maine' café-au-lait macules, unilateral bone lesions, asynchronous ovarian cysts) and the wide variability from isolated monostotic fibrous dysplasia to full multisystem disease (earlier mutation = more tissues affected). Because it is not germline, MAS is essentially not inherited and recurrence risk is negligible. A practical consequence: confirmatory GNAS testing should be done on affected tissue, not peripheral blood, where the mutation may be undetectable.
keyPoints: Post-zygotic somatic mutation → mosaic; only descendant cells affected.
Explains patchy/asymmetric features and variable severity.
Not inherited (negligible recurrence); test affected tissue, not blood.

items.title: Why MAS puberty is episodic, unlike testotoxicosis
items.content: The autonomous estradiol in MAS comes from functional ovarian cysts that form, enlarge, and rupture or involute, then re-form — so estrogen fluctuates rather than running continuously. Clinically this produces waxing-and-waning breast development and irregular, often heavy estrogen-withdrawal vaginal bleeding (which can be mistaken for menarche), with no progression to ovulatory cycles until bone age reaches the pubertal range or the disease burns out. This episodic pattern is the clinical fingerprint of MAS and contrasts sharply with testotoxicosis, where constitutive LH-receptor activation drives steady, continuous testosterone and progressive virilization.
keyPoints: Cyst cycle (form → rupture → quiescence → re-form) makes estrogen fluctuate.
Waxing-waning thelarche + irregular estrogen-withdrawal bleeds = the MAS fingerprint.
Contrast: testotoxicosis is continuous (steady testosterone), not episodic.

items.title: How one Gs-alpha defect causes multisystem endocrinopathy
items.content: Because Gs-alpha is the universal transducer for many hormone receptors, constitutive activation in different cell lineages produces different autonomous excess syndromes from the identical mutation. Beyond ovarian GIPP, MAS can cause: non-autoimmune hyperthyroidism (autonomous, no TSH-receptor antibodies); ACTH-independent Cushing syndrome (often neonatal and sometimes cyclic); GH excess (acromegaly/gigantism); polyostotic fibrous dysplasia (present in ~98%); café-au-lait macules (coast-of-Maine borders); and FGF23 overproduction by dysplastic bone causing renal phosphate wasting and hypophosphatemic rickets/osteomalacia. MAS is therefore a syndrome of multiple potential hormone excesses, not a single endocrinopathy.
keyPoints: Same GNAS defect in different tissues → different autonomous excess.
Thyroid (non-autoimmune hyperthyroidism), adrenal (ACTH-independent Cushing), pituitary (GH excess).
Bone (fibrous dysplasia ~98%), skin (café-au-lait), FGF23 → hypophosphatemia.

items.title: The classic triad and how to recognize MAS
items.content: The classic triad is café-au-lait macules (large, irregular 'coast of Maine' borders, often respecting the midline) + polyostotic fibrous dysplasia + gonadotropin-independent precocious puberty — but only about 30-40% have all three at diagnosis, so partial presentations are common. Practical rule: any girl with early vaginal bleeding plus suppressed gonadotropins, especially with café-au-lait macules or bone pain, should trigger a MAS work-up — examine the skin and screen the bones, and confirm with GNAS testing on affected tissue.
keyPoints: Triad: café-au-lait (coast of Maine) + polyostotic fibrous dysplasia + GIPP.
Only ~30-40% have all three — partial presentations are common.
Early vaginal bleeding + suppressed gonadotropins + café-au-lait/bone pain → work up MAS.

items.title: How to treat MAS precocious puberty (aromatase inhibitor first-line)
items.content: GnRH agonists are ineffective because the ovary is autonomous (the drive is downstream of the pituitary). First-line is a potent aromatase inhibitor, letrozole, which blocks the final step of estrogen synthesis, lowers estradiol, slows bone-age advancement, and reduces cyst recurrence. If insufficient, add an estrogen-receptor antagonist — tamoxifen (a SERM, but it can occasionally enlarge cysts via partial agonism, so monitor) or fulvestrant in refractory cases. Surgery (cystectomy/oophorectomy) is strongly discouraged because cysts recur, and is reserved for torsion or life-threatening haemorrhage. Add a GnRH agonist only if a mixed central component develops. Treatment is suppressive, not curative; many girls improve over years. Concurrent endocrinopathies (thyroid, GH, cortisol, phosphate) must be treated alongside.
keyPoints: GnRH agonists ineffective; letrozole (aromatase inhibitor) is first-line.
Add tamoxifen/fulvestrant if insufficient (tamoxifen may enlarge cysts — monitor).
Avoid surgery (cysts recur); add a GnRH agonist only if a central component appears.

items.title: How to monitor MAS: multisystem, lifelong surveillance
items.content: MAS demands broader, lifelong surveillance than isolated GIPP because GNAS can 'light up' any Gs-alpha tissue at any time. Puberty (every 3-6 months while active): estradiol, pelvic ultrasound for cyst size/number, bone age (every 6-12 months), growth velocity and Tanner stage. Other systems: skeleton — fibrous dysplasia imaging, fractures, and craniofacial involvement (vision/hearing); phosphate/FGF23 for hypophosphatemic rickets; thyroid (TSH, free T4); pituitary (IGF-1, GH); adrenal (cortisol, ACTH); and LFTs while on an aromatase inhibitor. Do not stop monitoring once puberty is controlled — GH excess, thyroid disease, and fibrous dysplasia can emerge years later, and care is multidisciplinary.
keyPoints: Puberty: estradiol, pelvic US (cysts), bone age, growth/Tanner every 3-6 months.
Screen thyroid, GH/IGF-1, cortisol/ACTH, phosphate/FGF23, and fibrous dysplasia.
Lifelong, multidisciplinary — new endocrinopathies can appear years later.

items.title: MAS versus testotoxicosis: the key contrasts
items.content: Both are gonadotropin-independent, but they differ on almost everything else. Defect: MAS is a post-receptor GNAS (Gs-alpha) mutation; testotoxicosis is an LH-receptor (LHCGR) mutation. Genetics: MAS is somatic mosaic and not inherited; testotoxicosis is autosomal dominant, male-limited. Sex: MAS affects girls far more than boys; testotoxicosis affects only boys. Hormone dynamics: MAS gives episodic, cyst-driven estradiol; testotoxicosis gives continuous testosterone. Associated features: MAS has café-au-lait, fibrous dysplasia, and multisystem endocrinopathy; testotoxicosis has none. Treatment: MAS uses letrozole +/- a SERM; testotoxicosis uses an anti-androgen + aromatase inhibitor (+/- ketoconazole).
keyPoints: MAS = post-receptor Gs-alpha, mosaic, girls, episodic estrogen, multisystem.
Testotoxicosis = LH receptor, AD male-limited, boys, continuous testosterone, isolated.
MAS: letrozole +/- SERM; testotoxicosis: anti-androgen + aromatase inhibitor.

items.question: Why are GnRH agonists ineffective for MAS precocious puberty, and what is used instead?
items.answer: GnRH agonists work by suppressing pituitary LH and FSH, which controls central precocious puberty. In MAS the estrogen comes from an autonomously active ovary driven by the constitutive GNAS/Gs-alpha-cAMP signal, entirely downstream of the pituitary — LH and FSH are already suppressed, so there is nothing for a GnRH agonist to switch off. Treatment must therefore act on estrogen itself: a potent aromatase inhibitor (letrozole) is first-line, blocking the final synthetic step to lower estradiol, slow bone-age advancement, and reduce cyst recurrence; an estrogen-receptor antagonist (tamoxifen or fulvestrant) is added if needed. A GnRH agonist is reserved only for the minority who later develop a mixed central component.
keyPoints: GnRH agonists suppress the pituitary, but MAS is autonomous and downstream of it.
Treat the estrogen directly: letrozole (aromatase inhibitor) first-line.
Add a SERM/ER antagonist if needed; GnRH agonist only for a later central component.

items.question: How does MAS require broader monitoring than other forms of precocious puberty?
items.answer: Because the same GNAS mutation can constitutively activate any Gs-alpha-coupled tissue, MAS is not one endocrinopathy but a potential of several, appearing at different times. So alongside the puberty work-up (estradiol, pelvic ultrasound for cysts, bone age, growth and Tanner staging every 3-6 months), surveillance must cover the whole system: thyroid (TSH, free T4) for autonomous hyperthyroidism, the GH axis (IGF-1, GH) for acromegaly/gigantism that further accelerates bone age, the adrenal (cortisol, ACTH) for ACTH-independent Cushing, phosphate and FGF23 for hypophosphatemic rickets from dysplastic bone, and skeletal imaging for fibrous dysplasia and its complications (fractures, deformity, optic/auditory compression). Monitoring is lifelong and multidisciplinary because new manifestations can emerge years after the puberty is controlled.
keyPoints: Multisystem because GNAS can activate any Gs-alpha tissue, at any time.
Screen thyroid, GH/IGF-1, cortisol/ACTH, phosphate/FGF23, and the skeleton, in addition to puberty.
Lifelong and multidisciplinary; manifestations appear at different ages.

items.stem: What is the molecular cause of McCune-Albright syndrome?
options: A somatic activating GNAS mutation (e.g. R201H/C) that abolishes Gs-alpha GTPase activity, causing constitutive cAMP
items.explanation: MAS is caused by a post-zygotic somatic activating GNAS mutation (commonly R201H/C) that removes the Gs-alpha GTPase 'off switch', locking Gs-alpha on and driving constitutive, ligand-independent cAMP signalling.

items.stem: Why is precocious puberty in MAS gonadotropin-independent?
options: Constitutive cAMP drives autonomous granulosa-cell aromatase/estradiol production independent of FSH, and the high estradiol suppresses LH/FSH
items.explanation: The ovary makes estradiol autonomously via constitutive GNAS/cAMP signalling without needing FSH, and the resulting high estradiol suppresses LH and FSH — peripheral (gonadotropin-independent) precocity.

items.stem: Why is MAS precocious puberty episodic rather than continuous?
options: Because LH pulses come and go
Because functional ovarian cysts form, rupture/involute, and re-form, making estradiol fluctuate with irregular withdrawal bleeding
items.explanation: Autonomous estradiol comes from functional cysts that cycle (form, rupture, quiescence, re-form), so estrogen fluctuates, producing waxing-waning thelarche and irregular estrogen-withdrawal bleeds — unlike the steady testosterone of testotoxicosis.

items.stem: Which statement about the inheritance of MAS is correct?
options: It is a post-zygotic somatic mosaic mutation that is essentially not inherited; confirm GNAS on affected tissue, not blood
It is always detectable in peripheral blood
items.explanation: MAS results from a post-zygotic somatic GNAS mutation, so it is mosaic and essentially not inherited; because unaffected tissues (like blood) may lack the mutation, testing should target affected tissue.

items.stem: What is the first-line treatment for MAS-induced precocious puberty?
options: A potent aromatase inhibitor (letrozole), since the ovary is autonomous and GnRH agonists are ineffective
items.explanation: Because the ovary is autonomous (downstream of the pituitary), GnRH agonists fail; an aromatase inhibitor such as letrozole is first-line, lowering estradiol and slowing bone-age advancement, with a SERM/ER antagonist added if needed.

items.stem: What is the classic triad of McCune-Albright syndrome?
options: Café-au-lait macules, polyostotic fibrous dysplasia, and gonadotropin-independent precocious puberty
Salt-wasting, ambiguous genitalia, and short stature
items.explanation: The MAS triad is café-au-lait macules (coast-of-Maine borders), polyostotic fibrous dysplasia, and gonadotropin-independent precocious puberty — though only ~30-40% have all three at diagnosis.

items.stem: Hypophosphatemia in MAS most often results from which mechanism?
options: FGF23 overproduction by dysplastic bone causing renal phosphate wasting
items.explanation: Dysplastic (fibrous dysplasia) bone in MAS overproduces FGF23, which drives renal phosphate wasting and can cause hypophosphatemic rickets/osteomalacia.

items.stem: Hyperthyroidism in MAS is best characterised as:
options: Autoimmune Graves' disease with TSH-receptor antibodies
Autonomous, non-autoimmune (no TSH-receptor antibodies), from constitutive Gs-alpha activation
items.explanation: MAS hyperthyroidism is autonomous and non-autoimmune (no TSH-receptor antibodies), driven by constitutive Gs-alpha/cAMP signalling in the thyroid — it should not be confused with Graves' disease.

items.statement: MAS is caused by an inherited germline GNAS mutation.
items.explanation: False. MAS arises from a post-zygotic somatic (mosaic) GNAS mutation and is essentially not inherited.

items.statement: MAS precocious puberty is episodic, with cyst-driven estrogen fluctuation and irregular vaginal withdrawal bleeding.
items.explanation: True. Functional ovarian cysts that form and rupture cyclically make estradiol fluctuate, producing waxing-waning thelarche and irregular withdrawal bleeds — the MAS fingerprint.

items.statement: GnRH agonists are the first-line treatment for MAS precocious puberty.
items.explanation: False. The ovary is autonomous and downstream of the pituitary, so GnRH agonists are ineffective; an aromatase inhibitor (letrozole) is first-line.

items.statement: Café-au-lait macules in MAS characteristically have irregular 'coast of Maine' borders.
items.explanation: True. MAS café-au-lait macules are large with irregular 'coast of Maine' borders, often respecting the midline.

items.statement: Because MAS is mosaic, GNAS testing is best performed on affected tissue rather than peripheral blood.
items.explanation: True. The somatic mutation may be undetectable in unaffected tissues such as blood, so affected tissue is preferred for confirmation.

items.statement: Once MAS precocious puberty is controlled, multisystem monitoring can be stopped.
items.explanation: False. MAS is lifelong; GH excess, thyroid disease, Cushing, hypophosphatemia, and fibrous dysplasia can emerge years later, so surveillance must continue.

items.assertion: GNAS mutation testing for suspected MAS should be performed on affected tissue rather than peripheral blood.
items.reason: MAS arises from a post-zygotic somatic mosaic mutation, so the mutation may be undetectable in unaffected tissues such as blood leukocytes.
items.explanation: Both statements are true and the reason explains the assertion. Because mosaicism means only descendant cells of the mutated clone carry the change, sampling affected tissue (rather than blood, which may be unaffected) maximises the chance of detecting the GNAS mutation.

options: A) Because the velocity of progression, not the onset, suggests an autonomous pathological driver such as a tumour or CNS lesion
B) Because normal onset indicates a benign, physiological process with no risk to final height
C) Because genetic factors are the most common cause and require no further investigation
D) Because psychosocial stressors are the primary cause and can be managed conservatively

items.stem: In a boy with precocious puberty, which testicular examination finding most strongly suggests a central cause (e.g., gonadotropin-secreting tumor) or an hCG-secreting tumor?
options: A) Bilateral testicular enlargement (≥ ~4 mL) with symmetry
B) Asymmetric or unilateral testicular enlargement with a palpable mass
C) Prepubertal-sized testes despite clear virilization
D) Absence of testicular enlargement with elevated testosterone levels
items.explanation: Bilateral testicular enlargement indicates gonadotropin or hCG-driven stimulation of both testes, which is characteristic of central precocious puberty or hCG-secreting tumors. Asymmetric enlargement suggests Leydig cell tumors, while prepubertal testes with virilization point to adrenal sources or exogenous androgens.
